coredata.dc:title: Carbon nano-structures and functionalized associates: Adsorptive detoxification of organic and inorganic water pollutants

Kumari, Kavitha
coredata.dc:description: Deterioration of water quality is a desperate threat worldwide. Industrial development regularly added a vast amount of organic and inorganic contaminants to water. Therefore, eradicating these contaminants is highly essential for the well-being of biotic components. Adsorption-assisted technologies are among the most benign and principally used due to their higher efficiencies at lower costs and independence of complex technological supports. Carbon nanomaterials, such as activated carbons, carbon nanotubes, graphene-based materials, and carbon dots have been broadly adopted as adsorbents because of their outstanding surface characteristics. Researchers worked a lot on activation procedures and chemicals to achieve an ultrahigh surface area (above 3000 m2/g) to increase the adsorption efficiency. Further, cross-linked carbon nanotubes exhibited much-selected adsorption to organics. Carbon dots, a new type of carbon-based nanomaterials emerged as excellent adsorbents when introduced into the polymers matrix and provided promising platforms for the removal of metal ions. Also, graphene oxide and other oxidized forms of carbon being rich in functional groups are reported to exhibit excellent adsorption capacities as high as 2564 mg/g at 25 °C for basic and cationic compounds. Here, we reviewed the various types of pollutants found in aquatic biota with some basics on fundamentals and mechanistic features of adsorption, adsorption efficiency regulating factors. The article emphasized the performances of carbon nanomaterials and associated nanocomposites for the adsorption of various pollutants. The review also addresses the essential issues for the technical development and commercial application of carbon-assisted materials as nano adsorbents for water decontamination.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Deterioration of water quality is a desperate threat worldwide. Industrial development regularly added a vast amount of organic and inorganic contaminants to water. Therefore, eradicating these contaminants is highly essential for the well-being of biotic components. Adsorption-assisted technologies are among the most benign and principally used due to their higher efficiencies at lower costs and independence of complex technological supports. Carbon nanomaterials, such as activated carbons, carbon nanotubes, graphene-based materials, and carbon dots have been broadly adopted as adsorbents because of their outstanding surface characteristics. Researchers worked a lot on activation procedures and chemicals to achieve an ultrahigh surface area (above 3000 m2/g) to increase the adsorption efficiency. Further, cross-linked carbon nanotubes exhibited much-selected adsorption to organics. Carbon dots, a new type of carbon-based nanomaterials emerged as excellent adsorbents when introduced into the polymers matrix and provided promising platforms for the removal of metal ions. Also, graphene oxide and other oxidized forms of carbon being rich in functional groups are reported to exhibit excellent adsorption capacities as high as 2564 mg/g at 25 °C for basic and cationic compounds.

Here, we reviewed the various types of pollutants found in aquatic biota with some basics on fundamentals and mechanistic features of adsorption, adsorption efficiency regulating factors. The article emphasized the performances of carbon nanomaterials and associated nanocomposites for the adsorption of various pollutants. The review also addresses the essential issues for the technical development and commercial application of carbon-assisted materials as nano adsorbents for water decontamination.

ce:section.ce:para: Pure water is considered one of the essential elements for all living creatures to survive on earth [1]. Though 71% of the surface of earth is water-covered, only 2.5% is freshwater, and the remaining 97.5% is present as saline water. In this way, merely 0.007 % of total water is available for drinking purposes [2,3]. Unfortunately, all the available water resources are being contaminated globally due to modern lifestyle demands, which is one of the biggest challenges in the present times. Water gets polluted in numerous ways, including sewage, mining activities, marine dumping, oil leakage, leakage from sewer lines, burning of fossil fuels, and other industrial activities [4-6]. Among the various possible reasons for water pollution, industrial wastes are considered the significant contributors to polluting water resources. Generally, industrial effluents compose different types of organic and inorganic materials such as pesticides, dyes, fertilizers, heavy metals, phenols, pharmaceuticals, radioactive elements, salts, and so on [7-9]. These polluting substances lead to congenital disabilities, debilitate the immune system, jeopardize the growth of children, and thus cause serious threats to all the aquatic and living species [10]. To handle this situation, researchers have developed several treatment methodologies that remove or degrade these harmful substances present in water. There are various methods based on the physical, chemical, and biological processes, which are extensively applied to minimize the water contaminants like adsorption, irradiation, membrane filtration (reverse osmosis, nanofiltrations), photocatalytic oxidation, biological degradation, and ion-exchange [11,12]. Membrane filtration methods are reliable and inexpensive techniques with no chemicals and can eliminate 90–100% of pathogens from the contaminated water sample. But, cracking or breakage (especially glass filters) of these filters is one of the generally faced problems. Also, only liquids can be sterilized, and turbid water cannot be used in these methods [13]. Selective and high removal efficiency is possible by choosing the suitable exchanger in the ion-exchange technique, but resin can be sensitive to pollution and oxidizing substances. Also, efficacy may reduce due to fouling and high disposal costs in the regeneration step, which are the main disadvantages of this method [14]. Biological treatment methods are environmentally safe and friendly, but large amounts of sludge or bio-solids are produced in the digestion that requires disposal. Although biological treatment is efficient in removing most of the organic contaminants, studies indicate that some chemicals, such as detergents, pharmaceuticals, cosmetics, and industrial compounds, remain still after biological wastewater treatment [15]. Advanced oxidation and photocatalytic reactions rapidly mineralize and degrade several molecules without emissions of dangerous substances. However, some technical constraints, laboratory scale, low throughput, and pH dependence are the major issues faced in these techniques [16].

Among the different approaches, adsorption is widely accepted and regarded as a coherent and economically- accessible technique. Herein, we emphasize the adsorption of carbon-associated materials to remove and minimize different kinds of pollutants from contaminated water. Some basics on fundamentals and mechanistic features of adsorption and adsorption efficiency regulating factors are also discussed. This article also addresses the essential issues to be considered for the technical development and commercialization of carbon-assisted nano adsorbents in the future for water decontamination.

ce:section.ce:section-title: A rational view of aquatic contaminants
ce:section.ce:para: Generally, various types of organic and inorganic pollutants are found in aquatic biota, affecting all life forms if not treated properly [17]. Organic contaminants include dyes, humic substances, pesticides, herbicides, petroleum products, phenolic compounds, surfactants, pharmaceuticals, drugs and many more [18,19]. On the other side, inorganic pollutants consist of mainly heavy metal ions, rare earth elements, and different oxyanions (for example, CrO4 2–, NO2 –, NO3 –, BrO3 –, ClO4 –, ClO3 –, and ClO2 –), halides, and radioactive materials.

Among the various organic contaminants, dyes the coloring substances are widely applied in different industries like food, paper printing, textile, and cosmetics to impart beautiful shades and are the primary contributing organic moieties. Nearly 7 × 107 tons of dyestuff are produced annually worldwide, which counts more than 100,000 dyes that are available commercially [20]. In textile production, around 12–20 tons per day, nearly 1000–3000 m3 of water is released in effluents after dye processing [21]. The discharged liquids mainly consist of inorganic and organic compounds that remained unfixed to the fabric during processing and, after that, were liberated in nearby water bodies leading to serious threats to all the aquatic and living species.

At present, agrochemicals may also be placed in the list of significant contaminants in which pesticides are the primary ones. Undoubtedly, pesticides significantly enhance crop production by controlling pests, but their excessive usage has terrifying impacts on living creatures [22]. The principal source of contamination from pesticides is poor management, especially on-farm dealing of pesticides and industrial effluents, and agricultural runoff [23]. Other than dyes and pesticides, herbicides, petroleum products, phenolic compounds, surfactants, pharmaceuticals, different kinds of drugs, including antibiotics, anti-depressants, and steroids, are the organic moieties that are present in wastewater and adversely affecting all living creatures, human health, and the economy [24-27]. Table 1 displays the different classes of organic contaminants along with their applications.

Inorganic contaminants include heavy metals, rare earth elements, different oxyanions (for example, CrO4 2–, NO2 –, NO3 –, BrO3 –, ClO4 –, ClO3 –, and ClO2 –), different halides, and radioactive materials. Waste effluents from mining, tanneries, organic chemicals, pesticides, pharmaceuticals, metal processing units, rubber and plastics are the primary sources of these metal pollutants. The wastewater from industries contains lead, selenium, mercury, chromium, uranium, arsenic, zinc, cadmium, cobalt, gold, silver, and nickel as the significant metal contaminants [42-44]. Table 2 displays the different inorganic contaminants with sources and various health impacts.

It is well known that adsorption is the adherence of any material to the surface of a substrate. It is a surface phenomenon in which interactive forces between the surface and incoming molecules depend upon the involved species’ nature. Broadly, the adsorption process is normally classified as physisorption or chemisorption. Physical adsorption generally implies thick multimolecular layers on the adsorbent, while in chemical adsorption, usually monolayers are formed. Electrostatic interactions, hydrophobic interactions, hydrogen bonding, π- π interactions, and complexation are the commonly occurring forces and depend upon the particular adsorption system [61].

In real practice, it is most convenient to determine the adsorption at a constant temperature; therefore, adsorption isotherm provides various technical information about the adsorption system. Adsorption may be seen as an effective tool for treating polluted water, particularly when the adsorbent is low-priced and there is no need for extra pre-treatment steps before the application [62]. Additionally, adsorption is considered superior to other purification techniques regarding simplicity and flexibility of design, starting cost, insensitivity towards toxic pollutants, and not producing noxious substances [63].

The adsorption efficiency of the adsorbents materials is regulated by surface area, particle size, amount of adsorbent, target compounds, pore structures, surface interactions, contact time, pH, and temperature conditions [63,64].

The surface charge characteristics of the adsorbents are well predicted by the pH of medium [65]. Literature reveals that in both the acidic and basic reaction mediums, the pollutant removal ratio may be maximized [66,67]. The point of zero charge (PZC), a critical parameter, tells about the nature of charge on the adsorbent material depending upon the pH of the reaction solution. The values of pHpzc are related to the oxygen content present in the material. The value of pHpzc decreases when the amount of oxygen in the sample increases. When we adjust the solution pH to values lower than the pHpzc, a positive charge is developed on the surface of the adsorbent and favors the electrostatic interactions of the negatively charged adsorbate molecules with the adsorbent surface. While, at a solution pH higher than pHpzc, the available functional groups get deprotonated, and the surface becomes negatively charged, which is ultimately suitable for the adsorption of positively charged species. Therefore, by changing the medium pH, we can alter the adsorption efficiency of the material significantly. For example, graphenic materials prepared via the modified Hummers Method [68] exhibit pHpzc values in the range 3.17–4.82 and showed the best results for the adsorption of Reactive Red 2 dye when the solution was adjusted to a pH = 2. As already described, at this pH below the pHpzc of the synthesized carbon materials, electrostatic interactions occur between the positively charged carbon surface and negatively charged dye molecules. In addition to electrostatic forces, hydrogen bonding and π-π interactions are also involved in the adsorption mechanism. Fig. 1 depicts the proposed adsorption mechanism for Reactive Red 2 dye on the graphitic materials at pH = 2.

The adsorption capacity and mechanism involved are determined by the nature and strength of the forces operating between the adsorbate molecules and the adsorbent. Adsorption may be physical or chemical according to the involved interactive forces. When weak and reversible interactions like diffusion, hydrophobic interactions, π-π stacking, dipole–dipole, hydrogen bonding, and van der Waals forces are present, adsorption takes place via physical mode. On the other hand, if irreversible and strong forces are present like redox reactions, complex formation, covalent bonding, and proton displacement, chemical adsorption occurs with the formation of a monolayer [69]. Different types of physical and chemical interactions possible with the adsorbents are shown in Fig. 2 .

For example, adsorption of different sulphonamides over the CNTs exhibited high adsorption efficiency and followed the chemical mechanism for adsorption [70]. The π-π type of interactions are usually dominant for the benzene ring containing compounds adsorbed on carbon materials. For instance, adsorption of triclosan [71], tetracycline, sulfamethoxazole, tyrosine [72], bisphenol A [73] on CNTs and graphene- like structures followed the π-π interaction mechanism. The molecules containing more number of aromatic rings interact strongly with the CNTs, while the compounds with a lesser number of aromatic parts result in weaker π-π interactions with the adsorbent. When pH of the reaction medium differs from the isoelectric point (where the surface is neutral), some charge is developed on the surface of the nano adsorbent. Simultaneously, ionizable contaminant gets protonated or deprotonated at different pH values and acquire positive or negative charges. Thus the resulting species interact via. electrostatic forces and are responsible for the adsorption and removal of the pollutant [74]. Hydrogen bonding also greatly contributes to the adsorption of organic pollutants onto carbon materials. Hydrogen bonds can be formed between the oxygen functional groups of the adsorbate with the aromatic rings of the carbonaceous surface. The adsorption of 17β-estradiol and 17α-ethinylestradiol on graphene oxide is mainly attributed to hydrogen bonding owing to the presence of –OH groups on the surface [75]. Hydrophobic interactions are another way of adsorption on carbon materials when the adsorbing molecules are hydrophobic organics [76]. These forces are dominant when the resultant charge on the carbon surface is zero, and the contaminant is present in its molecular form. Adsorption of sulphonamides on CNTs in different pH conditions is mainly controlled by hydrophobic forces [77]. Similar types of non-electrostatic hydrophobic forces are also reported between tetracycline and MWCNTs [78].

Surface area and particle size are the important parameters in the adsorption process that significantly affect the adsorption capacity of the material. Adsorption rate generally varies directly with the surface area and inversely with the particle size. There are several reports where adsorption capacity increased with surface area. For example, adsorption of metronidazole [79] on graphene-based material exhibited an almost linear trend with surface area. The synthesized graphites showed high specific surface areas (100–500 m2/g) and adsorption capacities in the range of 64–223 mg/g. The high surface areas were attributed to the areas induced by the agglomerations of particles, stacking up of graphitic sheets, and the small particle sizes of the material. Another report by Q. Jianhua [80] also observed the similar results in which surface areas of 2097.51 m2/g were obtained for porous biochar, and maximum uptake of 202.61 mg/g was achieved for Cr (VI) ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Initial dye concentration, amount of adsorbent, and temperature
ce:section.ce:para: The adsorption capacity of any surface is determined by the active sites available on it and the initial concentration of the dye solution. Generally, with an increase in initial dye concentration, dye removal efficiency decreases. This may be because of the saturation of active adsorption sites on the adsorbent. On the other hand, if the increase in dye concentration leads to an increase in the uptake capacity of the material, it is attributed to the significant driving force for mass transfer at high dye concentration [81]. For example, adsorption of methylene blue dye on cellulosic olive stones (green and black) [82] showed the highest dye removal at the initial concentration of 400 ppm when a complete range from 50 to 1000 ppm was studied. Fig. 3 shows the effect of dye initial concentration on the % removal for both black and green olive stones.

Fig. 3 clearly shows that at initial concentrations (below 400 ppm), lower % removal is attained and is explained on the basis of less mass transfer of the dye. When concentration increased from 300 to 400 ppm, the mass transfer force for the dye increased, and ultimately a percentage of adsorption also increased. Above 400 ppm (particularly at 600 ppm), available binding sites do not support mass transfer since the dye molecules occupy them. Thus, available sites decreased and resulted in lower % removal of dye at high initial concentrations.

Next, amount of the adsorbent is also an important factor that decides the adsorption capacity of the compound under given operating conditions. Usually, with increase in the adsorbent dosage, dye removal percentage increases as the number of sorption sites increase with increasing the amount of the adsorbent [83]. The study of the effect of adsorbent amount on adsorption capacity gives us an idea about the adsorption efficiency of any material towards a particular contaminant. It is therefore helpful from the economical point of view. Temperature is another influential parameter that influences the uptake capacity of the adsorbent. Adsorption may decrease or increase with temperature depending on whether the process is endothermic or exothermic. If the increase in temperature leads to increase in adsorption, it indicates an endothermic process. This is ascribed to the increase in the number of active sites in the material and increased mobility of the adsorbate molecules at higher temperatures. On the other hand, decrease in adsorption with temperature suggests exothermic adsorption. This is attributed to a decrease in the adsorptive forces between active sites and contaminant molecules, thus reducing the adsorption performance of the material [84,85].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carbon nanostructures in adsorption: Surface and structural characteristics
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon nanostructures are a group of captivating materials which exhibit an indispensable role in nanotechnology. Noticeably, scientific discoveries in this area have received great esteem for getting the Nobel Prize for the discovery of fullerene by Sir Harold W. Kroto, Richard E. Smalley, and Robert F. Curl in 1996 and for the development of free-standing graphene monolayers by A. Geim and K. Novoselov in 2010. Carbonaceous materials constitute a collection of structures that possess unique properties resembling the exclusive atomic structure of carbon atoms [86]. As long as size, shape, and dimensionality are considered, carbon materials are classified as zero-dimensional (carbon dots, Buckminster fullerenes), one-dimensional (carbon nanofibers and nanotubes), two-dimensional (graphene-based materials), and three-dimensional nanomaterials (carbon sponges). Carbon nanostructures having different dimensionalities, including 0D, 1D, 2D, and 3D carbon structures, are depicted in Fig. 4 .

These nano-sized materials bear high surface area, wide pore sizes, proper thermal stability, and different functional entities [93,94]. In light of these characteristic properties, carbon- associated nano-structured materials have received great attention from the scientific research community and made them potential candidates in environmental science. In the present article, we will focus on the adsorption characteristics of the carbon nanostructures and their associates and their efficiency toward water pollutant decontamination.

Activated carbon is a traditional, low-cost, and most popular form of carbon. In broad sense, it includes a variety of amorphous carbon-based compounds that actually possess a micro-crystalline structure, i.e. these materials are termed as ‘disordered materials’ when their order of porosity is considered and also ‘ordered materials’ when viewed on nano-scale. Moreover, it exhibits outstanding porous structure, high surface area, and adjustable surface properties [95]. These characteristic properties of activated carbon materials make them highly suitable for adsorption and removal of several types of contaminants present in water. In porous activated carbons, existence of small-scale crinkles and tiny crevices in different layers are helpful in generating higher number of active sites [96]. To make ready-to-act carbon materials, the first step is the carbonization of the crude material below 800 °C in an inert atmosphere and the second one is the activation of the product formed. During the first step, all the non-carbon elements present in the starting material get eliminated in the form of volatile gases, and residual carbon atoms arrange themselves in the form of cross-linked aromatic sheets in a disordered manner. This type of irregular arrangement of carbon sheets leads to free interstices, which promote pore formation and thus make activated carbons superb adsorbents. These pore structures are further developed and increased in the activation step and give rise to extend as well as the extremely high surface area of the synthesized material.

As per the International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry (IUPAC), pores are classified on the basis of their width, and broadly three groups are proposed, namely macropores (diameter >50 nm), mesopores (diameter 2 to 50 nm), and micropores (diameter <2 nm). The micropores contribute about 95% of the total surface area in activated carbon whereas the contribution of mesopores being 5% of the total surface area. On the other hand, macropores contribute little and are not of considerable importance in adsorption since their contribution to surface area hardly exceeds 0.5 m2/g [97]. Therefore, micropores are the dominating sites to determine the adsorption capacity of activated carbon provided the adsorbate molecules possess dimensions in the range of micropores.

No doubt, porosity of activated carbon materials is detrimental factor but the surface chemical structures also play a crucial role and strongly influence the adsorption capacity. It is widely recognized that the removal of chemical species by activated carbons is mainly attributed to the complex formation of the surface functional groups with the adsorbing species. It is observed that on activated carbons, oxygen containing carbonyl groups are the predominant functional groups. To introduce these functional groups, both dry as well as wet oxidation methods have been employed in literature. Dry methods involve treatments with oxidizing gases like CO2, steam at high temperatures whereas wet methods involve reactions with oxidizing solutions like HNO3, H2SO4, NaOCl, H2O2, and (NH4)2S2O8 under mild conditions [98-100]. In this direction, acidic treatments are found beneficial regarding adsorption of metal ions, but also detrimental to pore volume and surface areas. For example, Maroto-Valer et al. [101] observed reduced surface area and pore volume on treatment with HNO3, where blockage with extra oxygen complexes was the reason for decreased parameters. A similar type of behavior was also reported by Aburub and Wurster [102]. They explained that the demolition of porous structure within activated carbon resulted reduction in surface area and pore volume owing to oxidation by nitric acid. Since conventional oxidizing agents lead to a decrease in surface area, researchers have tried alternative methods which introduce functional groups only on the external surface and without affecting the internal structure of the adsorbent. Oxygen plasma treatment is one of such techniques and has been found successful in a number of reports [103,104]. Later on, it was recorded that this plasma hypothesis does not work in all experimental situations and may vary according to the design of the experimental set up [105]. Activated carbons can behave as acidic, basic as well as neutral surfaces depending upon the functional groups that are present on the surface. For example, the presence of lactone, carboxyl, phenol and lactol type of groups impart acidic nature to the surface whereas chromene, pyrone, diketone and amine groups provide basic characteristics to the carbon surface [106]. Therefore, in conclusion, we can say that carbon exhibits a unique character in which porosity controls the adsorption capacity and chemical structure actually decides the interactions of different polar or nonpolar adsorbate molecules with the carbon surface.

In line with the above discussion, it is clear that in the physical mode of activation, we obtain carbons of good porous structure, but longer activation times required in the synthesis and lower adsorption capacities of the synthesized products are the main disadvantages of this treatment method [107]. It is known that different chemicals (like H3PO4, ZnCl2, K2CO3, NaOH, KOH) can also be used as activators for carbons that produce different properties in the resulting activated carbons. These activators may be added into the precursor either through physical mixing in dry conditions or by impregnation under wet conditions [108]. The activated carbons generated by chemical activation methods exhibit more porous structures than those produced from physical mode [109,110]. Generally, cellulosic and lignocellulosic substances are activated with ZnCl2, H3PO4 wherein highly polar nature of phosphoric acid controls the physical and chemical interactions taking place in the impregnation stage and ZnCl2 acts as wetting agent during activation. Activation via. H3PO4 mainly proceeds through dehydration, depolymerization, and reorganization of biopolymers in lignocellulosic materials [111,112] and finally results in highly porous structures. ZnCl2 produces an electrolytic effect known as swelling in the cellulosic moiety and inflation ultimately breaks down the cellulose molecules. Thus, higher surface areas are produced due to the development of inter and intra coated cavities [113]. Similarly, other than NaOH and KOH, K2CO3 is also a popular activating agent for the production of activated carbons. The leading reactions that may happen between K2CO3 and carbon during the activation step are presented below.
K2CO3 + 2C → 2 K + 3CO
K2CO3 → K2O + CO2
K2O + 2C → 2 K + CO

Thus, reduction of K2CO3 during activation in inert conditions to produce K, CO2, K2O, and CO is responsible for the development of porosity in the material [114]. The potassium compound generated during this stage penetrates the char matrix, expands the already existing pores, and creates new pores. Among alkali materials, KOH gives better results than NaOH regarding surface area and performance in various applications. However, NaOH, being cheaper, environmental friendly and less harmful than KOH, has superior industrial applications over KOH [115].

Literature reported several works that describe adsorption of different kinds of drugs, dyes, pesticides, and inorganic pollutants from water onto the surface of activated carbons. D. Franco et al. [116] removed diclofenac using activated carbon and found Freundlich isotherm as the suitable one to describe the adsorption behavior and confirmed multilayer adsorption. Some others including triclosan [117], atenolol [118], salicylic acid, phenol, and methylparaben [119], ampicillin [120], and salicylic acid and acetaminophen [121] and many more compounds have been removed by using commercial as well as modified activated carbons derived from varying sources.

In addition to the conventional activated carbons, as mentioned above, magnetic activated carbons are also effective and impressive adsorbents for waste aqueous solutions [122,123]. These type of materials have a special advantage that they can be easily drawn off from the solution with the help of magnetic separator. A number of magnetic carbon composites have been employed to treat different kinds of organic as well as inorganic water contaminants. Reguyal et al. [124] and Zhao et al. [125] used magnetic biochar and removed sulfamethoxazole and Pb (II) ions respectively. A Fe3O4@polyaniline composite was used for the adsorption of decabromodiphenyl ether and tetrabromobisphenol A [126]. Other than these, magnetic components like Fe3O4, MFe2O4 (M: Co, Cu, Mn, Ni), and α-Fe2O3 also have been loaded on activated carbons and the resulting composites utilized to remove pharmaceuticals, perfluorinated compounds, antibiotics, metal ions, and dye pollutants [24,127-131].

A unique and highly efficient composite adsorbent based on activated carbon and 3D- network of ZnO tetrapods was proposed [132] and successfully employed for decontamination of hexavalent chromium from aqueous solution. Sugarcane bagasse was used to prepare activated carbon via chemical activation and carbonization. The proposed material integrated the individual properties of ZnO and activated carbon like high porosity, large surface area with abundant functional groups, non-toxicity, and durability towards harsh conditions; thus, make it a unique adsorbent with great potential for heavy metal removal.

Table 3 summarizes activated carbons, modified activated carbons, and their composites applied for various decontaminants. Ionic liquids are also explored in contaminant removal through adsorption by the synthesis and functionalization of carbon-based adsorbents and definitely improved their adsorption behavior. 1-butyl-3-methylimidazolium bromide was used to impregnate chitosan beads [127] and the prepared conjugated beads were capable of removing methylene blue dye, showing 86% removal efficiency in just 25 min.

Graphene materials are the two dimensional layered structures of single-atom thickness and have sp2 hybridized carbon atoms. The graphene sheets behave as conductors owing to zero band gap and it is difficult to adsorb organic species onto the graphene surface which is hydrophobic in character [160]. In this direction, several treatment methods like doping, modification with different functional groups, and composite formation with metal oxides have been implemented to improve the characteristics of graphene structures [161-164]. Usually, graphene is used as graphene oxide in water treatment applications. Graphene oxide is generally prepared via an acid treatment process by following a popular Hummers method [165]. Such a treatment induces a number of oxygen containing polar functional groups into the graphene layers, mainly carboxyl, carbonyl, hydroxyl and epoxide groups [166]. The introduced functional groups being hydrophilic in nature allow them to disperse in water and thus beneficial in water flow across the graphene layers leading to enhanced electrostatic interactions. That is why graphene oxide is considered as a promising substrate for preparing various graphene-based nanocomposites.

Adsorption mechanism and capacity of graphene and graphene-based materials rely on the type of interactions between graphene material and the adsorbing contaminant species. For example, whether hydrogen bonding, electrostatic forces, or π-π interactions are present in between interacting species will determine the actual adsorption capacity [167]. Generally, organic molecules and polymers interact with graphene oxide via van der Waals forces, while π-π interactions are established with the molecules exhibiting a π-system. Oxygen bearing functional groups present on the edges and surface of graphene layers allow ionic and hydrogen bonding interactions [168,169]. Fig. 5 depicts different types of interactions that are possible between naphthalene, naphthol, and Cd2+ ions with the graphene adsorbent.

For example, heavy metals being positively charged can be effectively adsorbed over the graphene oxide which behaves as anionic surface due to the presence of π-electron cloud in the graphene layers. The adsorption capacity for different metal ions varies according to the electronegativity of the metal, standard reduction potential of the respective metal ion, as well as the first stability constant of the corresponding metal hydroxide [171]. The metal ions with higher electronegativity strongly attracted toward the graphene oxide surface. In the same trend, higher values of stability constant and reduction potentials favor the adsorption phenomenon.

Heavy metal adsorption on the surface of graphene materials occurs via electrostatic forces, surface complex formation, or ion exchange routes [172]. Adsorption following electrostatic interactions has already been described in the same section. Complexation of metal species with the oxygen containing functional groups on the graphene surface is another possible way for adsorption. Next, the protons of the oxygenous groups (–OH, –COOH) can be exchanged with the metal ions and provide a mechanism for adsorption. Sometimes, electrostatic repulsions dominate over the π-π interactions as observed in the case of phenols as the adsorbing species onto graphene oxide. That is why the surfaces having lesser number of oxygen groups (like reduced graphene oxide) or positively charged functional groups (like polypyrrole) are found to exhibit the best adsorption capacities for phenols [173-175].

Although pristine graphene acts as an efficient adsorbent for several organic and inorganic pollutants, modified graphene structures with nanomaterials [176], biomolecules [177], polymers [178], and surfactants [179] are also found as excellent adsorbents with enhanced adsorption performance. Graphene oxide sponge possessing macro-hierarchical structures was found to be an efficient purifying agent for the remediation of cadmium ions in aqueous environment [180]. The composite was prepared by integrating graphene oxide, titanium isopropoxide, and xanthan gum via a facile freeze-casting approach and water was taken as the pore forming agent. The adsorption mechanism mainly involved complexation and electrostatic attractions of the cadmium ions with the oxygenated functional groups of the adsorbent.

Literature reveals a large number of studies devoted to metal oxide modified graphene materials for adsorption of water pollutants [181-183]. The inclusion of metal oxide particles into the graphene layers limits their aggregation and re-stacking, hence increasing the surface area of the composite. Many metal oxides including ZnO, Fe3O4, TiO2, SiO2, MnO2, ZrO2, CoFe2O4 [184-191] have been hybridized with graphene and are found to be effective adsorbents toward different water contaminants. A highly stable and magnetically separable GO/Fe3O4/Pd nanocomposite was synthesized [192] and showed high activity for Methylene blue, Methyl Orange, Congo red and 4-nitrophenol adsorption as well as reduction. Recently, nitrate and phosphate ions were removed by using graphene oxide - Fe3O4 composite and found different interactions responsible for the adsorption of these inorganic pollutants as shown in the Fig. 6 .

Numerous reports constitute graphene oxide composites with different kinds of materials including magnetic particles, metal oxides, mixed metal oxides which showed good adsorption for various pollutants like rhodamine B, methyl orange, methylene blue, neutral red, remazol black, dimethoate, chlorpyrifos, glyphosate, and many heavy metal ions as summarized in the Table 4 . C.-X. Chen et al. [194] studied the role of graphene in adsorption of sulfadiazine antibiotic over the AQ2S@rGO nanocomposite where the existing meso- and macropores in the material offered effective adsorption sites for the pollutant. Also, grafting different functional groups into the graphene structure assisted in adsorption via noncovalent interactions and increased the affinity between anthraquinone-2-sulfonate and graphene structure.

Carbon dots are the zero dimensional nanomaterials which were discovered for the first time by Xu et al. [218] during the purification of carbon nanotubes. The exclusive properties of carbon quantum dots like chemical stability, aqueous solubility, easy fabrication and functionalization differentiate them from normal semiconductor quantum dots [219,220]. Generally there are two types of carbon based dots namely carbon quantum dots and graphene quantum dots. Carbon quantum dots are the small quasi-spherical particles possessing size in the range of 2–10 nm consisting of almost diamond-like carbon insertions [221]. On the other hand, graphene quantum dots are composed of sp2 hybridized carbon atoms surrounded by the domains which exhibit better crystallinity than the carbon quantum dots [222].

Carbon quantum dots can be synthesized by using both top-down and bottom-up approaches. Top-down approach includes the breaking of larger structures like graphite with the help of methods like laser ablation, arc-discharge, and electrochemical oxidation [223-225]. Whereas bottom-up strategy uses chemical precursors like hydroxy acids, carbohydrates, and polymersilica nanocomposites to prepare carbon dots via combustion, ultrasonication, thermal treatments, and microwave-assisted routes [226-229]. According to literature, a number of chemical precursors are reported that produce carbon quantum dots (CQDs) including glucose, citric acid, ammonium citrate, ascorbic acid, food wastes, humic acid, and gelatin [230-236]. Among all these materials, citric acid is the most popularly used starting material that has been employed in carbon quantum dots fabrication. These days, biomass is also regarded as a green source to synthesize carbon quantum dots. Natural precursors like grass [219], ginkgo leaf [237], pomelo fruit [238], broccoli [239], dried leaf [240], wood soot [241], lemon and onion [242], rice-husk [243], bio-waste lignin [244] have been reported in literature that produce carbon quantum dots by following different synthesis techniques among which hydrothermal method is considered the most facile and economical route [245]. It is important to note that the precursor and the synthesis method must be selected carefully since both these may affect the surface properties as well as the hybridization of carbon atoms present in the carbon quantum dots [246].

The adsorption mechanism varies according to the adsorbing species, whether they are atoms, ions, or molecules. In case of organic molecules, π-π interactions of aromatic part of the molecule with the conjugated sp2 structure of carbon quantum dots are responsible for the molecular contact in aqueous medium [247,248]. Whereas, heavy metal ions and several other molecules interact with the surface of CQDs through different functional groups present on their surfaces like carbonyl, hydroxyl, carboxyl, epoxy and amino groups. Generally, carboxylic moieties are found on the surfaces of carbon dot materials where the oxygen content may vary according to the synthesis procedure used [249]. The presence of such moieties improve water solubility as well as provides reactive chemical groups on the surface for further functionalization. These materials being in the nano range, exhibit a high surface area to volume ratio and thus provide extra active sites on the surface for the adhesion of molecules.

Recent reports have focused on heteroatom doped carbon quantum dots to enhance the electronic as well as optical properties of the materials. Doping of heteroatoms involves surface modification where the doped atoms behave as supports for the carbon dots [250]. In non-metals doping, doping of nitrogen (N) is the most commonly employed method due to similar size of N atoms to the carbon atoms. Literature indicates a number of reports where N-doping is found effective in improving the characteristics of carbon quantum dots. For example, Hu et al. [251] used four different N-sources to prepare doped CQDs and found enhanced quantum yield. N-doped carbon nanodots obtained from carboxy methylcellulose [252] showed 83% removal efficiency for Cu2+ ions in just 30 min. Another study reported N-doped carbon quantum dots [219] as good candidates for removal of Cd2+ and Pb2+ ions from water. The particles obtained were smaller than 10 nm in diameter and thus resulted in high surface area. In addition to non-metal doping, some reports also recorded metal-doping in carbon dots and found to improve their electrical, optical, and chemical properties. In the study by Wang et al. [253], the Bi-doped carbon quantum dots were proven to have good electron transfer properties when compared to other metal dopants. K. Chung Hui [243] studied comparison between metal-doping and non-metal doping of carbon quantum dots towards MB degradation and Cu (II) removal. CQDs were derived from rice husk where ethylenediamine and bismuth nitrate pentahydrate were taken as the nitrogen and bismuth sources respectively. The N-doped carbon dots sample (10 vol%) gave a removal performance of 56.23% while only 33.13% removal of copper (II) was achieved by the Bi-doped carbon dot sample (5 wt%).

Moreover, the N atoms and oxygen bearing functional groups found on the surface of carbon dots enabled metal ions capture by providing active sites for adsorption. Shuyi Huang and co-workers [254] synthesized magnetic as well as fluorescent carbon quantum dots functionalized with polyethyleneimine groups. The prepared nanocomposite showed 91% removal of U (VI) ions from aqueous solution. Further, the functional groups present (–OH, –NH2, –COOH) were found responsible for the increased adsorption and were realized by the formation of outer-sphere complexes on the adsorbent surface.

Apart from that, different kinds of aerogels including silicon, carbon, organic polymers, and metal oxides are the materials possessing large surface area, low density and thermal conductivity. Among them, nanofibrillated cellulose aerogels are of particular interest because of their low price, eco-friendliness, and wide range of raw materials available. Z. Song et al. [255] constructed nanofibrillated cellulose aerogels to support carbon quantum dots. The prepared composite was successfully applied for the detection and adsorption of Cr (III) ions. Adsorption of the metal ions was detected by observing the fluorescence intensity of CQDs. Fig. 7 depicts adsorption, detection and optical pictures for the Cr (III) ions over the prepared aerogel composite.

Graphene quantum dots are also used successfully as adsorbents for the removal of several toxic water pollutants [256,257]. For example, 95.70% removal of pesticide, Oxamyl, was obtained by graphene quantum dots, prepared from citric acid and following a microwave-assisted hydrothermal route [258]. Recently, Q. Zhou et al. [259] employed BiOCl nanosheets/carbonized eggshell membrane decorated with carbon quantum dots for removing Rhodamine B, tetracycline hydrochloride, and o-nitrophenol. The synthesis route of the prepared nanocomposite is shown by the scheme as shown below in the Fig. 8 . It is disclosed that the novel hierarchical structure and the {001} exposed facet of BiOCl are the dominant factors that are responsible for the increased adsorption. The adsorption of different water pollutants using the carbon and graphene quantum dots are displayed in the Table 5 .

Carbon nanotubes are the novel, one dimensional, cylindrical shaped nanostructures in the form of rolled graphene sheets. The graphene sheets entirely constitute sp2 hybridized C-C covalent bonds which are stronger than the C-C bonds present in diamond and therefore, account for the unique properties of these structures [265]. These are classified as single walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs) and multi walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) which consist of a single cylindrical tube of carbon atoms and multiple concentric nanotubes respectively [266,267]. SWCNTs possess lengths varying from 50 nm to 1 cm and diameters from 1 to 2 nm while MWCNTs have wider diameters in the range 10–100 nm [268]. The structural characteristics of CNTs like length of the tube, number of walls, diameter, angle, and chirality actually decide the area of application of these materials [269]. These one dimensional nanomaterials exhibit high surface area, high electrical conductivity, rigid platform structure, possibility of easy derivatization with several molecules due to which this class of materials is widely useful in various fields and applications including adsorption, photocatalysis, sensors, drug delivery agents, hydrogen storage, and field-effect transistors [270-274].

Carbon nanotubes provide high surface area, and good quality active sites that facilitate adsorption over these materials [275]. These materials possess a special hollow structure that account for better stability of the composite formed and longer retention time of adsorbed molecules on the adsorbent [276].

Initially, carbon nanotubes were obtained by using laser ablation and arc-discharge methods which usually require high temperature synthesis conditions. However, these days lower temperature chemical vapor deposition allows the formation of such nanomaterials [269]. For example, Iijima [277] discovered multi walled carbon nanotubes which were produced by arc-discharge method at 3500 °C and using graphite electrodes. Later on, carbon nanotubes were prepared at much lower temperatures around 700–950 °C with the help of chemical vapor deposition process [278].

The adsorption mechanism in case of CNTs is found similar to that of activated carbons due to similarity in carbon skeleton. However, in case of activated carbons the adsorbate must diffuse into the internal porous structure, but it is not a bounding factor in case of CNTs. For larger adsorbate molecules, the surface of CNTs not interior is the principal site for interactions. SWCNTs permit multiple molecules to load across the length of the nanotube exposing all the atoms on the surface owing to high surface area [268]. Further, binding of aromatic compounds onto the surface of CNTs is attained by π-π interactions.

Carbon nanotubes in their pristine form are hydrophobic in nature. The high aspect ratio in these materials allows van der Wall interactions between the particles where no steric and electrostatic forces are present to balance these out [265]. When present in suspension, CNTs form mesoscopic aggregates comprising around 50–100 strongly entangled carbon tubes [279]. In aggregated state, CNTs exhibit micro and meso pore sites internally for adsorption which are suitable especially for smaller molecules [280]. Mechanical untangling of carbon nanotubes is generally obtained by ultrasonication where the basic chemical π-network and intrinsic properties remain unchanged except that the length of carbon nanotubes gets shortened.

The available surface area and the functional groups present on the surface of CNTs are the main determining factors which effect adsorption capacity [281]. Functionalization of carbon nanotubes is carried out with the help of chemical reactions, usually oxidative processes (utilizing any oxidising agent like nitric acid) thus arising sp2 reactive carbon atoms in the form of OH and COOH groups on the surface which are again involved in further chemical reactions [282]. The oxidised CNTs are water soluble, but aggregation takes place in the presence of salts and are not usable for certain applications. Some other modifications comprise of using hydrophilic polymers and cycloaddition reactions [268,283] that disturb the conjugation of the carbon system and hence affects the optical as well as electrical properties of the carbon nanotubes. Functionalization of CNTs not only enhances the adsorption capacity for pollutants, but is also helpful in making composite materials. Several polymers including polyaniline, chitosan, polysulfone, and polyacrylamide have been tailored for adsorbing contaminant species [284-289]. In addition to polymers, CNTs have been coupled with solid supports like aluminium, silica, ceramics and metallic nanoparticles to facilitate water purification [290-293]. The performance of various CNTs based materials regarding adsorption of different water contaminants is summarized in Table 6 . A bifunctional and magnetic polysilsesquioxane/carbon nanotubes composite was prepared by sol–gel method [294] and utilized for removing Hg (II) ions from aqueous solution. The optimal pH for removal was found to be 4.5 and adsorption capacities varied from 1.63 to 1.94 mmol/g as per the Langmuir isotherm model. Moreover, the regeneration tests showed that the adsorption efficiency could still reach 78% after three repeat cycles and thus the composite was capable to remove even low concentrations of Hg (II) from waste water. Bentonite‑CNTs composite [295] was found effective adsorbent towards the simultaneous adsorption of heavy metals and organic micro pollutants. The composite exhibited microporosity and high specific surface area leading over 90% removal of anthracene, benzotriazole, Zn2+ and Pb2+ ions. It is known that graphene and graphite oxide are 2D-flaky nanomaterials whose large surface area, high mechanical strength, and rich adsorption sites make them excellent adsorption materials [296]. Recently, the combination of graphene materials with CNTs to prepare 3D- composite aerogels has aroused great attention in the area of adsorption. For example, porous graphene/carbon nanotube composite prepared through hydrothermal reaction [297] was observed a promising 3D- porous adsorbent for MB dye.

The adsorption mechanism can be predicted with the help of adsorption isotherms, thermodynamic parameters, and kinetic models. Several isotherm models have been proposed and extended to decide adsorption behaviour i.e. whether monolayer or multilayer and homogenous or heterogeneous adsorption takes place. Some important ones include Langmuir, Freundlich, Temkin, Sips, Dubinin-Radushkevich, Jovanovich, Redlich-Peterson, Flory-Huggins, and Khan [319-328] where Langmuir, Freundlich, and Dubinin-Radushkevich are the most commonly employed methods to study the adsorption isotherms. The Langmuir adsorption model explains monolayer adsorption over the homogeneous surfaces and computes maximum adsorption capacity (mg/g) of the adsorbents [329]. Freundlich model explains adsorption via. multilayers formation in the case of heterogeneous surfaces [321]. The Dubinin-Radushkevich model estimates mean free adsorption energy of the adsorbents (J/mol). The energy values less than 8 suggest physical adsorption, from 8 to 16 correspond ion-exchange, and from 16 to 40 indicate chemical adsorption [181].

The thermodynamic parameters like standard enthalpy changes (ΔH°), free energy changes (ΔG°), and standard entropy changes (ΔS°) play important role in deciding the occurrence and favourability of the process. The Van’t Hoff plot is used to calculate ΔH° and ΔS° values which in turn give the ΔG° values. The different spontaneity criterion of the thermodynamic parameters (ΔH°, ΔG°, and ΔS°) are further used to determine the behaviour of the overall process [330].

To investigate the rate of adsorption, different kinetic models have been proposed like pseudo-first or second-order, inter or intra-particle diffusion, and Elovich models. The pseudo-first order kinetic model explains the physical interactions at the initial stage while chemical interactions are described with the help of pseudo-second-order model [331]. The diffusion process takes place mainly in three successive steps (1) bulk diffusion, (2) film diffusion where migration from the bulk occurs to the adsorbent surface across the boundary, and (3) intra-particle diffusion (pore diffusion) that occurs from outer parts into the pores of the adsorbent [332].

Thus, thermodynamic parameters, adsorption isotherms, and kinetic models can clarify the spontaneity and favourability along with the physical or chemical characteristics of the adsorption process. The adsorption mechanism of different organic and inorganic pollutants onto carbon-based materials is shown in the Fig. 9 .

Water pollution is a major concern these days for everyone - researchers, society, industries – together with the policy-makers at national as well as international levels. Removal of different kinds of organic and inorganic contaminants from wastewater is a top priority. In this direction, adsorption achieves adequate results in the eradication of several kinds of water contaminants. Adsorption is influenced by multiple factors, especially nature of the adsorbate molecules along with the adsorbent surface, strength of interaction forces and some other variables such as temperature, pH, surface area and particle size. It is regulated by thermodynamic parameters, adsorption isotherms, and kinetic models, and principally happens via physical, chemical, and ion-exchange mechanisms.

Recently, among the vast number of adsorbents that have been employed for the removal of toxic water contaminants, carbon nanostructured materials including activated carbons, CNTs, carbon dots, and graphene based materials have earned pervasive attention. This review article summarizes surface and structural characteristics of different carbon assisted materials as well as the adsorption performances of these materials for numerous organic and inorganic molecules such as dyes, pesticides, heavy metal ions, different kinds of drugs, and pharmaceuticals.

Pristine carbon nanostructures exhibit high adsorption performance for hydrophobic and aromatic molecules but, their modification and functionalization definitely improved the adsorption capacities for a broad spectrum of contaminants. The chemical (H3PO4, ZnCl2, K2CO3, NaOH, and KOH) activation routes results to the formation of extremely porous carbon structures. Graphene-based materials and carbon dots due to their abundant functional groups, unique optical properties, and good biocompatibility serve as outstanding materials for several organics and inorganics removal. Although, carbon-assisted materials have achieved admirable credibility in recent decades, but large scale production of carbon nanomaterials (especially CNTs and graphene based adsorbents) is still expensive. These materials are not found cost-competitive when compared with the activated carbons. Thus efforts are required to decrease the process costs. Next, the mitigation of the secondary wastes in adsorption is highly desired. The adoption of circular path approach may be a way to solve the problem, wherein residue from a process may act as resource for another. Further, more attention has to be given on following issues in the future investigations:
•
Novel and innovative synthesis methods to minimize the production cost of carbon associated nanomaterials and improvement in their adsorption capacity.
•
Utilization of biowaste-derived carbon nanomaterials for waste-water treatment.
•
Manipulation of animal residues such as eggshell, bone, and bristles for the synthesis and application of carbon nanomaterials in water treatment.
•
Exploitation of natural materials like clays, zeolites as cost-effective, abundant, and nontoxic supports for the synthesis of the nanomaterials and their application in water treatment.
In closing, it is assumed that the carbon nanomaterials will achieve amazing characteristics with regular investigations to act as highly efficient adsorbent for the purification of water.
